title: Incident Bot v2 operator guide + troubleshooting (deploy/config diff/saturation cards)
preview: This PR updates the operator-facing documentation for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (GA-ready). The goal is to make the bot usable in the first 5 minutes of an incident by standardizing what each context card means, how to validate it, and what to do when it’s missing/empty due to permissions or upstream timeouts. The...
text: This PR updates the operator-facing documentation for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (GA-ready). The goal is to make the bot usable in the first 5 minutes of an incident by standardizing what each context card means, how to validate it, and what to do when it’s missing/empty due to permissions or upstream timeouts. The guide also documents degraded-mode behavior (cached context + links) and the security model for tenant-safe config diffs (redaction + audit logging + break-glass). Scope: docs only (no product/runtime changes). Motivation: recent incident retros and the beta rotation feedback showed that responders could see the cards but weren’t sure what thresholds/links were authoritative, and we repeatedly re-taught the same troubleshooting steps in #help. This adds a single canonical “Operator guide” that Support/SRE/On-call can link during active incidents. Contents: (1) Overview of ...

title: Add TP stall dashboard panels + alert rules (heartbeat gaps, stall reasons, timeout correlation)
preview:
Context
We’ve had repeated Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted incidents where TP replicas appear healthy at the process level but are effectively wedged: requests hang, streaming timeouts rise, and autoscaling thrashes. Runtime-side instrumentation is landing in parallel (ENG-4823 / runtime PRs), but we need a first...
text:
Context
We’ve had repeated Dedicated + high-throughput Hosted incidents where TP replicas appear healthy at the process level but are effectively wedged: requests hang, streaming timeouts rise, and autoscaling thrashes. Runtime-side instrumentation is landing in parallel (ENG-4823 / runtime PRs), but we need a first-class dashboard + alerting layer to detect silent stalls early and make triage actionable.

This PR adds:
- Grafana dashboard panels for TP heartbeat gaps (per rank + aggregated), stall reason counts, and request timeout correlation by model/cluster/replica.
- Prometheus alert rules with sane defaults + paging policy alignment (page only on sustained stall/timeout correlation, ticket otherwise).
- Links from alerts to the on-call runbook and to the most relevant filters (cluster/account/model).

What’s included (panels)
1) TP Heartbeat
- Panel: “TP heartbeat age (p50/p95/p...

title: Add Dedicated staged rollout dashboards + alert templates (stage p95/p99, error rate, GPU saturation, promotion annotations)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a first cut of dashboard + alert templates for the Dedicated staged rollout system. The dashboards are stage-aware (stage_id / stage_name) and are designed to support the rollout gate model in the policy engine (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health proxies). It also adds annotations...
text:
Summary: This PR adds a first cut of dashboard + alert templates for the Dedicated staged rollout system. The dashboards are stage-aware (stage_id / stage_name) and are designed to support the rollout gate model in the policy engine (latency, errors, saturation/headroom, GPU health proxies). It also adds annotations for stage promotion/abort/hold events so operators can correlate metric shifts with rollout actions.

Context / dependencies: This is intended to ship alongside the redwood monorepo changes that (a) emit stage tags in metrics/traces and (b) provide audit events for promote/abort/hold. Dashboards assume the following labels are present on the relevant metrics: dedicated_pool_id, rollout_plan_id, stage_id, stage_name, region. For cardinality safety, the dashboards intentionally avoid any per-request labels and only group-by the allowlisted stage identifiers.

What’s included...

title: Dedicated throughput SLO: add dashboard panels for floor tracking, saturation, and drilldowns
preview: Context: We’re rolling out a formal Dedicated throughput SLO and need a consistent, low-friction dashboard experience for SRE/CS and (later) a curated subset for customers. This PR extends the dedicated mixin dashboards with panels that (a) track throughput floors over time per customer/deployment, (b) show saturati...
text:
Context: We’re rolling out a formal Dedicated throughput SLO and need a consistent, low-friction dashboard experience for SRE/CS and (later) a curated subset for customers. This PR extends the dedicated mixin dashboards with panels that (a) track throughput floors over time per customer/deployment, (b) show saturation signals that correlate with SLO misses, and (c) provide drilldowns into the exact deployment/model/region slice.

What’s included: 1) “Throughput floor vs actual” panels: tokens/sec (per deployment_id) overlaid with computed floor (tier + shape) and SLO evaluator compliance; includes 5m/30m rollups with clear “below floor” highlighting. 2) “Saturation signals” row: admission-control shed rate, queue depth, in-flight requests, batcher wait time, KV cache hit ratio (when available), and GPU utilization proxy (SM occupancy / active time from node exporter where present). 3)...

title: Dedicated throughput SLO: add burn-rate alerts + wire to paging policies
preview: Context: As part of Dedicated throughput guarantees, we need first-class SLO alerting that is (a) burn-rate based (fast/slow), (b) scoped per Dedicated deployment, and (c) correctly routed to oncall + CS escalation paths. This PR adds burn-rate alert rules for the Dedicated Throughput Floor SLO and wires them into o...
text: Context: As part of Dedicated throughput guarantees, we need first-class SLO alerting that is (a) burn-rate based (fast/slow), (b) scoped per Dedicated deployment, and (c) correctly routed to oncall + CS escalation paths. This PR adds burn-rate alert rules for the Dedicated Throughput Floor SLO and wires them into our paging policies. It also adds the runbook links and dashboard deep-links to make the alerts actionable. Scope: - New Prometheus rules for Dedicated throughput SLO burn-rate (page + ticket) - Tier-aware routing labels (Standard/Plus/Premium) to support different urgency/paging - Alertmanager routes + PagerDuty service mapping (SRE oncall; non-page tickets to #customer-success triage) - Annotations: customer/deployment identifiers, region, model, and links to the Dedicated Throughput dashboard panels - Guardrails: suppress alerts when deployment is in provisioning/warmup w...

title: Runtime: safer TP defaults (concurrency + batch guardrails) behind feature flag
preview:
Title: Runtime: safer TP defaults (concurrency + batch guardrails) behind feature flag

Context
We have repeated reports of TP replicas hanging under sustained mixed prefill/decode + bursty concurrency (see SUP-48321, SUP-48344; also linked to ongoing ENG-4821/ENG-4824 work). While root-cause fixes are landing (sche...
text:
Title: Runtime: safer TP defaults (concurrency + batch guardrails) behind feature flag

Context
We have repeated reports of TP replicas hanging under sustained mixed prefill/decode + bursty concurrency (see SUP-48321, SUP-48344; also linked to ongoing ENG-4821/ENG-4824 work). While root-cause fixes are landing (scheduler ordering, NCCL stream sync, watchdog), we still need a low-risk operational mitigation we can roll out quickly to Dedicated canaries.

This PR introduces a feature-flagged guardrail set for TP replicas that reduces the probability of starvation / collective mismatch scenarios by:
- capping concurrent decode microbatches per TP replica
- limiting prefill batch tokens under mixed workloads
- bounding prefill queue depth / admission to avoid long prefill bursts starving decode + collectives

The intent is NOT to "solve" TP hangs, but to reduce incidence while deeper fixe...

title: Load-shed new requests when TP stall detector trips (degrade replica + redirect/reject)
preview: Problem: When a TP replica is wedged (collective stall / scheduler starvation / heartbeat gap), we currently keep routing new work to it until upstream timeouts/backpressure eventually trigger. This amplifies p99, increases queue depth, and can cascade into autoscaling thrash. This PR wires the TP stall signal into ...
text: Problem: When a TP replica is wedged (collective stall / scheduler starvation / heartbeat gap), we currently keep routing new work to it until upstream timeouts/backpressure eventually trigger. This amplifies p99, increases queue depth, and can cascade into autoscaling thrash. This PR wires the TP stall signal into overload protection so that once a replica is suspected-stalled it is immediately marked DEGRADING and stops accepting new requests; callers either get a fast, structured overload response or (when possible) are redirected to healthy replicas. Approach: 1) Introduce a "replica degraded" state driven by TP stall detector signals (from ENG-4823) with hysteresis to avoid flapping. 2) Teach the local admission controller + router to consult degraded state and load-shed before enqueue. 3) Surface a structured error code for clients and for internal retry logic, and export metric...

title: Runtime: TP watchdog timeout + safe abort/drain path for stalled steps
preview: Context: We have recurring Sev1/Sev2s where a TP replica silently stalls (most commonly during decode under mixed prefill/decode load). Today that manifests as requests hanging until upstream timeouts, with the replica continuing to accept work and compounding the blast radius. This PR introduces a feature-flagged w...
text: Context: We have recurring Sev1/Sev2s where a TP replica silently stalls (most commonly during decode under mixed prefill/decode load). Today that manifests as requests hanging until upstream timeouts, with the replica continuing to accept work and compounding the blast radius. This PR introduces a feature-flagged watchdog that detects “no forward progress” in TP execution and triggers a controlled abort + replica drain. Goal is to fail fast, drain safely, and prevent GPU wedging / queue pileups while we continue to harden scheduler + NCCL ordering. What this PR does: (1) Adds a TP step watchdog that monitors progress at two levels: per-request “decode iteration progress” and per-rank “collective step counter” (fed by existing TP step bookkeeping; see ENG-4823/PR-21834 for richer metrics). The watchdog is implemented as a lightweight timer in the TP execution loop with a monotonic clo...

title: Add TP stall fault-injection tests to validate watchdog + abort semantics
preview: Context: We’ve been hardening TP watchdog + abort/drain semantics (ADR-0127, ENG-4826) and adding stall detectors/metrics (ENG-4823). We didn’t have regression coverage that proves: (1) a rank/collective “silent stall” actually triggers the watchdog within the configured threshold, (2) the abort path returns the exp...
text:
Context: We’ve been hardening TP watchdog + abort/drain semantics (ADR-0127, ENG-4826) and adding stall detectors/metrics (ENG-4823). We didn’t have regression coverage that proves: (1) a rank/collective “silent stall” actually triggers the watchdog within the configured threshold, (2) the abort path returns the expected structured error, and (3) the replica drain/overload hooks fire so we don’t wedge GPUs indefinitely.

This PR adds a small fault-injection surface and an integration test suite that runs a multi-rank TP replica in-process (test harness spawns N ranks) and intentionally introduces controlled stalls. The goal is deterministic coverage without relying on real NCCL deadlocks.

What’s included:
- Fault injection knobs (test-only) to simulate the major stall classes we see in the wild:
 - "collective_never_completes": rank 0 blocks before a collective and never reaches the ...

title: Dedicated: tier-aware admission control guards (queue caps + overload shedding)
preview:
Context

Dedicated throughput guarantees require us to keep tail latency and per-deployment throughput stable even when a pool is oversubscribed (mis-sized customers, sudden bursts, noisy neighbors, or control-plane misroutes). Today, overload behavior is mostly “best effort” and can lead to queue buildup that drags...
text:
Context

Dedicated throughput guarantees require us to keep tail latency and per-deployment throughput stable even when a pool is oversubscribed (mis-sized customers, sudden bursts, noisy neighbors, or control-plane misroutes). Today, overload behavior is mostly “best effort” and can lead to queue buildup that drags the entire pool down.

This PR implements tier-aware admission control guards in the gateway->orchestrator path:
- queue caps per (deployment_id, route) with tier-specific ceilings
- overload shedding rules that preferentially shed lower-tier/burst traffic while protecting committed Dedicated throughput floors
- consistent, observable rejection semantics (429 vs 503) and reason codes

Links
- Linear: ENG-19257 (Tier-aware admission control)
- Related: ENG-19244 / PR-19321 (per-deployment throughput metrics), ENG-19263 / PR-19366 (tier->pool mapping)
- Support signal: SUP-1...

title: TP: harden NCCL collective stream/event synchronization (graph-safe, feature-flagged)
preview: Context: We have recurring TP hangs under sustained mixed prefill/decode load where one or more ranks appear to block indefinitely in/around NCCL collectives. In incident traces and NCCL debug logs (see SUP-48377), we consistently see patterns consistent with stream ordering/synchronization assumptions being violate...
text:
Context: We have recurring TP hangs under sustained mixed prefill/decode load where one or more ranks appear to block indefinitely in/around NCCL collectives. In incident traces and NCCL debug logs (see SUP-48377), we consistently see patterns consistent with stream ordering/synchronization assumptions being violated across compute/comm streams when prefill and decode interleave and when CUDA Graph capture is enabled for steady-state decode. This PR adds explicit stream/event synchronization around NCCL collectives to make the ordering guarantees deterministic and to prevent silent stalls caused by missing dependencies.

What this changes: 1) Introduces a dedicated NCCL stream per TP replica (existing) but enforces a consistent dependency contract: compute -> (record event) -> NCCL stream waits -> launch collective -> (record event) -> compute stream waits. 2) Adds a small helper (Ncc...

title: Add Private backup/restore dashboards + alert rules (backup success, duration, restore drill outcomes)
preview: Context: As part of the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit, we need an opinionated observability pack that SRE/Solutions can deploy into customer VPC/on-prem environments to answer two questions quickly: (1) are backups happening and completing successfully on schedule, and (2) are restore drills passing and meeti...
text: Context: As part of the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit, we need an opinionated observability pack that SRE/Solutions can deploy into customer VPC/on-prem environments to answer two questions quickly: (1) are backups happening and completing successfully on schedule, and (2) are restore drills passing and meeting RTO targets in staging. This PR adds a set of dashboards and alert rules designed to work across hosted VPC installs and constrained on-prem/air-gapped setups (where available metrics are limited and log shipping may be disabled). Scope: (A) dashboards for backup job success rate, last_success_ts, duration, bytes, and error categories; (B) dashboards for restore drill outcomes (pass/fail, duration, validation phase breakdown) and drill cadence; (C) alert rules with conservative defaults plus documented tuning for noisy environments (slow object storage, intermittent KMS ...
links: Linear: ENG-9826 (metrics + alerts), ENG-9827 (restore drill automation)

title: Improve NCCL error surfacing to API layer (actionable error codes + routing hints)
preview: Context: Under TP load, we’ve had multiple incidents/support escalations where replicas wedge in a NCCL collective (or watchdog aborts the step), but the request surfaces upstream as a generic deadline exceeded / internal error. That prevents (a) smart routing/fallback from kicking in quickly, (b) customers from get...
text:
Context: Under TP load, we’ve had multiple incidents/support escalations where replicas wedge in a NCCL collective (or watchdog aborts the step), but the request surfaces upstream as a generic deadline exceeded / internal error. That prevents (a) smart routing/fallback from kicking in quickly, (b) customers from getting actionable diagnostics, and (c) on-call from correlating API timeouts to a specific NCCL failure mode. This PR wires the runtime’s NCCL/TP failure signals (including watchdog-triggered aborts) through the internal RPC boundary to the API layer and normalizes them into stable error codes + routing hints.

What changed: 1) Introduced stable RuntimeErrorCode values for TP/NCCL failure modes (kept intentionally small + versionable): RWD_TP_NCCL_TIMEOUT, RWD_TP_NCCL_ABORTED, RWD_TP_NCCL_COLLECTIVE_MISMATCH, RWD_TP_NCCL_UNHANDLED, RWD_TP_REPLICA_DRAINED. 2) Runtime now captu...

title: Scheduler: guardrail to prevent TP collective starvation under prefill-heavy load
preview:
Context
We have a repeated hang/timeout signature in TP replicas under sustained mixed prefill/decode load where collectives become ready but never get scheduled promptly because the scheduler keeps selecting prefill-heavy work that maximizes token throughput. When one rank gets delayed on a collective boundary whil...
text:
Context
We have a repeated hang/timeout signature in TP replicas under sustained mixed prefill/decode load where collectives become ready but never get scheduled promptly because the scheduler keeps selecting prefill-heavy work that maximizes token throughput. When one rank gets delayed on a collective boundary while others enter the collective path, NCCL ordering diverges and we end up in a silent stall (or downstream request timeouts).

This PR adds a narrow scheduler guardrail: when a TP group has collective-ready work pending beyond a bounded window, we temporarily bias scheduling decisions to ensure the collective boundary is reached and executed across ranks. The goal is correctness/stability first, with minimal perf impact.

What changed (high level)
- Introduce the concept of "collective-ready" work items in the runtime scheduler for TP replicas.
- Track a per-TP-group "collec...

title: Fix deadlock on cancelled streaming requests in tensor-parallel decode
preview: Context: We’ve observed intermittent hangs/timeouts on TP replicas correlated with streaming responses during peak load. One reproducible signature is a client disconnect (or server-side cancellation) mid-decode that triggers an early-exit path on one rank while other ranks continue into the next NCCL collective. Be...
text:
Context: We’ve observed intermittent hangs/timeouts on TP replicas correlated with streaming responses during peak load. One reproducible signature is a client disconnect (or server-side cancellation) mid-decode that triggers an early-exit path on one rank while other ranks continue into the next NCCL collective. Because our TP decode loop assumes all ranks execute an identical sequence of collectives per step, this can leave some ranks waiting indefinitely in ncclGroupEnd()/allReduce while another rank has already unwound. This PR fixes the cancellation path to preserve collective ordering invariants across ranks and to fail fast rather than wedging the replica.

Repro: Added an integration repro that opens a streaming request, reads N chunks, then hard-closes the client connection (RST) and repeats under concurrency against a TP=4 replica with mixed prefill/decode. Prior to this cha...

title: Add deploy context card (recent deploys + canary status) to incident thread diagnostics
preview: Motivation: During recent incidents we repeatedly lost time answering “what changed?” in the first 5 minutes. This PR adds a new Incident Bot diagnostics card that attaches deploy context directly into the incident thread, including recent deploys for the affected service/region, rollout phase, canary health, and co...
text: Motivation: During recent incidents we repeatedly lost time answering “what changed?” in the first 5 minutes. This PR adds a new Incident Bot diagnostics card that attaches deploy context directly into the incident thread, including recent deploys for the affected service/region, rollout phase, canary health, and commit/image identifiers. It’s designed to be tenant-safe and useful even when deeper telemetry is timing out. Scope: (1) New Slack “Recent deploys” context card rendered in the thread reply alongside existing diagnostics cards. (2) Backend integration to fetch recent deploy metadata from the release registry (service -> last N deploy events) and normalize it into the bot’s context payload. (3) Links out to the deploy timeline view and the referenced commit/PR when available. UX notes: The card summarizes up to 3 most recent deploys in the last 6 hours, then provides a “View ...

title: router: apply route experiment selection before fallback policy evaluation
preview: Problem: When a route has both (a) a fallback policy (e.g., model fallback / region fallback) and (b) a route experiment (A/B variants), the router currently evaluates fallbacks first and only then attempts experiment selection. In practice that means experiments can be silently bypassed during mild capacity pressur...
text: Problem: When a route has both (a) a fallback policy (e.g., model fallback / region fallback) and (b) a route experiment (A/B variants), the router currently evaluates fallbacks first and only then attempts experiment selection. In practice that means experiments can be silently bypassed during mild capacity pressure or transient model health flips, and worse, attribution becomes misleading because traffic that should be split is instead routed via fallback and never hits the intended variant. This PR changes the routing order so experiment selection happens before fallback evaluation, while preserving safety behavior when experiment config is missing/invalid/unavailable. New order (high-level): 1) Resolve route + canonical route_id; 2) Evaluate eligibility + deterministic assignment for the experiment (if enabled and applicable) to pick a target variant; 3) Evaluate fallback policy f...

title: Backpressure controls + max lag safety (auto-pause) for downstream outages
preview: Context: We had a beta incident where Splunk HEC throttling (429 + intermittent 503) caused exporter lag to spike and sustained retries increased queue pressure and CPU, impacting other tenants sharing the same exporter shard (see #incidents thread 1739559010 and SUP-18531 follow-up). This PR introduces explicit bac...
text:
Context: We had a beta incident where Splunk HEC throttling (429 + intermittent 503) caused exporter lag to spike and sustained retries increased queue pressure and CPU, impacting other tenants sharing the same exporter shard (see #incidents thread 1739559010 and SUP-18531 follow-up). This PR introduces explicit backpressure controls and a max-lag safety mechanism to keep the system stable during downstream outages while preserving at-least-once semantics.

What’s in this PR: (1) Max lag threshold per destination with auto-pause: if export_lag_seconds for a (tenant, destination) exceeds configurable max_lag_seconds for a sustained window, we stop pulling new batches for that destination and mark it PAUSED (reason=MAX_LAG) while continuing to process other destinations/tenants. (2) Backpressure based on in-flight bytes and retry backlog: introduce per-destination in_flight_bytes limits...

title: Add dashboards + alerts for data residency enforcement (blocked requests, policy eval latency)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a first-pass set of dashboards and alerting rules for Hosted data residency enforcement. The focus is on (1) visibility into enforcement outcomes (allowed vs blocked), (2) early detection of regressions in policy evaluation latency, and (3) detecting cross-region routing attempts that should be...
text: Summary: This PR adds a first-pass set of dashboards and alerting rules for Hosted data residency enforcement. The focus is on (1) visibility into enforcement outcomes (allowed vs blocked), (2) early detection of regressions in policy evaluation latency, and (3) detecting cross-region routing attempts that should be prevented once enforcement is enabled. Context: As part of Hosted data residency enforcement rollout (region pinning + deterministic request-time blocking), SRE and Security asked for a consistent set of metrics visualizations and actionable alerts. This pack is intended to be deployed alongside the routing/gateway changes and used during dogfood and progressive rollout. What’s included: Grafana dashboard: “Data Residency Enforcement” with panels: Overview: total requests, allowed/blocked, block-rate (%), and top block reasons (5m/1h). Blocked requests by region + endpoint...

title: Add ExperimentConfig CRD + server-side validation for route experiments
preview: Context This PR introduces the initial ExperimentConfig schema (CRD-style config object) used by the control plane + policy engine to define route-level experiments (A/B/n). It includes strict server-side validation and user-facing error messages that Console can surface directly. This is the first building block fo...
text: Context This PR introduces the initial ExperimentConfig schema (CRD-style config object) used by the control plane + policy engine to define route-level experiments (A/B/n). It includes strict server-side validation and user-facing error messages that Console can surface directly. This is the first building block for deterministic bucketing as defined in ADR-047. Links Linear: ENG-9021 ADR: ADR-047-deterministic-experiment-bucketing What’s included Schema / model Introduces ExperimentConfig with: metadata: experiment_id, display_name, owner_team, created_by, description (optional) scope: account_id (tenant), environment, route_id(s) variants: variant_id, name, allocation (0..1), target (model_id + optional runtime overrides), enabled flag (default true) assignment: stickiness_key (enum), hash_seed, fallback_key_behavior, allow_missing_identifiers flag guardrails: optional thresholds (...
